title: Nathalie Schrans -Hiring Manager Screen 2 - Panzer | Technical Managing Editor

Hi, Matthew.
Hi, how are you?
I'm doing well, thank you.
How's your day been?
Very, very busy.
I can understand that.
Full of things.
Lots of context switching.
Just the startup stuff, you know.
I totally get that.
mean, just working at an agency, I feel like that's kind of how it goes.
Yeah, agency work is very similar.
It's always adjusting content.
Contexts and re-remembering which context in which you are working at that moment.
Yeah, re-remembering is a great way of putting it sometimes.
I'm like, oh, I've definitely done this.
I'm looking at my notes.
I'm like, I took these notes.
Okay.
Yeah, so happy to take some time to chat.
Hopefully, I'll leave a little time for you to ask me questions as well.
I know you've spoken to Marilia, so you've gotten a little bit of a view of the role and that sort of thing.
But I kind of want to ask a handful of questions and talk to you about it.
First off, just as a matter of a little bit of intro, you don't have to run me through your LinkedIn profile, but just kind of where you're at, where you're coming from, that kind of thing.
Just love to hear a little bit about you.
Yeah, so I've been in marketing for about 10 years now, specifically content marketing for, I think, a little over six years.
I've been...
I've been...
Mostly in B2B, especially on the content marketing side, although I do have some B2C experience.

And since I started my current role at Animals at an agency, I've had the opportunity to work with a ton of different B2B SaaS tech companies, mostly a little bit of B2C or sometimes a blend of the two.

So I've gotten a lot of experience working with like companies at different stages from early stage startup to, you know, more enterprise side where they have like pretty big internal marketing teams already.
Working across all different types of channels and content.
lot of my experience in the beginning, especially was more typical, like long form content, blog articles, data reports, white papers, that kind of thing.

But definitely throughout the years in content marketing, I've expanded into things like social media, email, kind of experimenting with different channels and trying to push beyond what we see as like typical content marketing, like SEO, which I think is a very strong.
Um, strategy for a lot of teams, but it's not always, I don't like to only focus on that when I think there's opportunities to do more.

Um, something I've also been working with AI tools and workflows for the past, I would say two and a half ish years, like starting early on experimenting with like ChatGBT, how can I find new efficiencies in my workflow?
We're just like replacing these, you know, repetitive manual tasks with AI things.
Um, so I have a lot of experience with building AI workflows from scratch, kind of identifying points where that could be solved by AI and also really like quality checking AI output.

So like making sure that, yes, of course, AI can help speed things up, but if what it's producing is not really good quality or straight up incorrect, like I've definitely come across things or made mistakes on my own and learned from them.
So I have a lot of experience kind of like fact checking and making sure that any sort of AI assisted content or things that AI produces, whether it's in like my own research, ideation, that kind of.
Making sure that that's high quality and also accurate and especially specific or like specific to the client's goals or marketing or brand voice, things like that.
Okay, cool.
And talk to a little bit more about some of the automation tools that you've used, specifically workflow or pipeline creation tools.
What have you played around with there?
I would say I've mostly been using ChatGPT, Cod, and not playing a little bit with Aerox.
So I also have some experience with Zapier, although that's not exactly the same as like AI workflows, but I have definitely experienced like building steps in a workflow.
The downside being, you of course, with ChatGPT and Cod, you're not building repeatable workflows in the same way like in Aerox, you like build a workflow and just run it.
So a lot of my experience is kind of, I guess, more on the like manual side of like basically building prompts, but like building a series of prompts.
So it's a work, like kind of like a little bit of a manual workflow.
So I do have experience building.
I understand that growthx, you have your own platform and workflows are built directly in the product.

So I know it's a little different from ChatGPT, but I would say that my experience, especially working in these products or with these tools for the past couple of years, I like, I know a lot about what they can and can't do, where you kind of have to like insert human interaction, like quality checks, rather than just letting AI kind of like continue or hallucinate or run it in a direction that isn't very helpful.
So I would say that's, even though I, most of my experience in building these AI workflows is pretty like manual type of prompt engineering or like building prompt library.
I would say that kind of helps me understand, like, where are the issues and why you can't rely on AI for certain things or need to like interrupt it before it continues in an unhelpful direction.
Yeah, yeah, sounds good.
I think, you know, if you have some familiarity with AIOps, I mean, you're...
You've been dabbling in an arena, at least, where you wouldn't find what we're doing to be too dissimilar, right?
At least initially, you know, it has more depth, so there's more water underneath the surface, you know, it's a deeper well that we're drawing from.
But on the surface, at the very least, if you've dabbled with a Aerox or N8N or Make.com or Zapier, you've got the general concept of what we're doing.
Definitely, yeah.

Yeah, the, and as you've found, like the non-deterministic nature of LLMs means that, yes, you know, they work in, when you have full context and you're doing Socratic editing, they can work pretty well.
Well, these obviously do require a lot of checks and balances and constriction of their tendency to wander, et cetera.
And part of what we're building into the workflows are those rails and are the directional.
just say, you.
Minus
And some of it is context engineering, some of it is prompt engineering, and then some of it is workflow or pipeline engineering.
This role, I think, is, it's certainly not a software engineering role, but it is one that really demands the ability to understand how a workflow processes inputs.
It produces outputs at a surface level, or probably a little bit subcutaneous.
So, like, it's very helpful if you have ever, like, built any workflow at all.
It's very helpful to have that knowledge.
And the more experience you have doing that, the more comfortable you're going to be.
Because the operators of the workflows, we treat the workflow as the writer, basically.
The workflow is doing the writing.
And the level above that is really, like, an assignment editor, you know, like a content manager.
You know, organization are really more like an assignment editor.
They're giving the writer an assignment and then they're watching them closely, like hovering over their shoulder.
Are you sure that's the way you want to go with this?
Are you sure?
And you probably have that experience with LLMs.
You know, you have to do.
Definitely.
Hover at them a lot.
I'll stop.
I'm like, please, no, this is not what I asked for.
No, no, I'll save the tokens because you're not going to use this.
Yeah, exactly.

And then the, of course, the workflows and pipelines themselves, theoretically, we hope, are being improved by the ENG team over time to require human interventions in service of like that ineffable essence of humanity that hopefully is going to polish these things the extra 10% or get them to where they need to be.
Right now, I would think we're probably a ways away from that goal, unfortunately, but that's just the way of the world.
know, that's the, that's the, the world we live in is, is very nebby.
And every base model upgrade that ships, our product gets better.
This is why AI startups are so crazy right now because it's like, hey, we're literally getting free upgrades to our capabilities.
This is very much like I'm a little bit long in the tooth when it comes to tech.
So this is very much like the first sort of cloud era when AWS was like, hey, cloud compute just got 50% cheaper.
And we're like, okay, cool.
My startup that was not going to work just became viable overnight.
Like there's a lot of that at that time.
I remember it very well.
And this feels a lot like that where it's like, oh, we've been really like struggling with this Gordian knot of an issue with our pipelines.
And then they're like, hey, we just upgraded our reasoning model and we run a test.
And we're like, well, that's solved.
You know, I think that's helpful.
Obviously, it's energizing in a way because there's billions of dollars being spent on helping you to fix your engineering problems that you don't have to pay for.
So.
So.
That part of it's good.

But it also means that the position demands, you know, nimbleness to understand, okay, hey, I understand the current array of inputs and outputs that we're working with, the dials that we have to twiddle here to get the proper outputs.

And I can step in and help, like, think architecturally about those so that my CMS don't get frustrated or that my clients are getting the deliverables they want, articulate clearly the ways that these workflows are falling down, the structures that need to be improved here.
And that's an important differentiation between somebody who is, like, an amazing content writer and can produce really clean copy and even energizing and fun and clever and on-message branding copy.
That's cool.
I'm happy.
You know, I'm happy that skill set is there.
However, the layer that we need that's above that is the ability to then articulate.
exactly why that is.
Why is it good?
Why is it bad?
Why is it on brand?
Why is it not?
Because we need to then take those to do the context engineering part of it, which is improve the artifacts that we use to inform the workflows for a particular client.
And then, of course, file tickets and get things fixed from the engineering perspective.
So it definitely requires a blending of those two worlds.
I would say the job is probably 60-40, workflow engineering versus content, because it flows backwards often.
You know, you'll get to a place where you're like, ah, this content's bad.
Okay, why is it bad?
Probably not because a CM doesn't know that it's bad.
They're like, oh, this stinks, you know, and that's why you're noticing in the first place.
That would be you going, okay, we'll just rewrite it or whatever.
But that's not our job.
Our job is to be able to go like, okay, what caused this?
What input may have caused this?
What, yeah, what component of the workflow caused this?
And then...
Rectify that, right?
So that's a big chunk of the job.
So in that vein, it does not have to be LLM related because nascent field, et cetera.

But can you talk to me a little bit about process improvements and how you've kind of recognized like a breakage in a process or something that wasn't being produced correctly and then how you discovered it and rectified it?
Yeah.
So I think a few months ago was when we really started building workflows in AirOps and testing that across our clients.
And so we built a workflow to basically go from keyword to article with multiple steps in between of like building a brief, building an outline and everything.
And what I noticed, I had already basically created my own process for doing this, assisted with AI.
But in AirOps, what I noticed was that the briefs and the outlines that it created just weren't really good or even relevant to like the target audience or like what the client was trying to do.
Specific to their, you know, messaging and positioning and everything, like their specific methodology.
So the, therefore the article wasn't really good and it wasn't really like helpful to the reader or including like specific examples that would have been useful.
And so I kind of like was investigating like where in this process were things going wrong?
And ultimately it came down to the brief, the brief, like not really being specific.

And something that I find to be really helpful in briefs is creating a structured outline that has details of like what every section should cover, suggests internal links to include, things like that.

And so kind of putting in a little more work in that part to make sure that we have a really strong outline so that the input, like as you're describing the input is much better and then naturally you will have better output.
And so that was where like kind of figuring out that that was, I would say probably like the biggest issue in the workflow and just improving the brief creation process.
like I said, I had already.
I'll And
I had my own process of like how to go from research to brief, to outline, to writing.
So working with the other person who was also building this workflow and sharing like, oh, this is how I've been doing my briefs.
Here are examples of like other briefs I've created based on this process.
And then articles I created, all of this, like using AI throughout, creating these articles based on these briefs and outlines.
And he was just really, I guess, he was like fairly new to using AI to build workflows.
And so I think he was just really impressed with this brief process and how you're able to, like, you can't just tell AI to write an article about something and expect the article to be good.
You have to, like you've said, the input has to be really good.
You have to have important, useful context, good research and inputs backing it up.
So by kind of sharing and explaining my process, especially the brief part, was able to improve these workflows overall.
And we kind of did a comparison of the original Aero Ops workflow, that article.
Versus my process and just having our editor and like other people on the team review it.
And there's just like a massive difference in quality, especially because I think another part of my brief writing process, especially when it comes to SEO, is really doing in-depth SERP comparisons.
So what do the top ranking pages include?
Just to give us an idea of like, okay, this is what people are looking for.
This is how the algorithm is rewarding these pages because they cover, you know, X, Y, Z topics.

Maybe we don't need to cover all 10 of these sections if it's not relevant, but we know these are kind of the most popular things and therefore what people tend to expect and what would probably be useful to them.
So that is definitely, I think, something that helped kind of rebuild this brief process and improve it rather than just kind of having a brief that wasn't very specific or detailed.
And even just recently, actually, earlier this week, that same editor came back and like, we kind of do like random quality checks.
Thank you.
Thank
content, especially now that we're doing so much AI-assisted content production.
So he mentioned that he randomly reviewed an article I wrote and then said, this brief should be studied.
Clearly, the brief was so good because the article was so well-written and well-done, even though I used AI in some way through every part of the production process.

So I would say, I know you said, oh, it doesn't have to be like an LLM specific example, but I would say just by nature of adopting these tools very early and kind of like understanding the problems that come with them.
And knowing that you can't replace everything that you do with AI has helped me learn where am I needed?
Where do I need to stop something or add my input or tell, like, sometimes I'll just be like, this is everything I have.
This is what I want you to do.
Please ask me all the questions you need.
If there's any information that's missing so that we can complete this task together.
And I think that really helps me kind of understand, like, just by nature of

That experience and knowing the problems that surface a lot with these tools, like understanding where I need to improve things or what opportunities there are, and maybe some areas where adding AI just wouldn't fix things.
would maybe introduce other problems down the line, especially with regards to quality.
Yeah.
Yeah, that's good.
I think that's a good articulation.
You're on the right track as far as we're concerned, because we found a lot of similar things.

Some of it is related to just the standard best practices when it comes to prompting or giving LLMs a task, but then some of it is also very specific to content, like understanding exactly how articulate you need to be in the brief in order for supportive research to be germane, to do the SERP analysis, but also not be led by the nose around by it, because you're going to make editorial calls about how you want to...
Yeah.

Or like what angle or what to focus on that's actually relevant to your target audience with the goals that we have, which is definitely, yeah, you can't just put everything, you can have a 5,000 word article and hope that it's good enough.
Well, you can.
Yeah, I guess people do that.
Yeah, the one problem LLMs do not have is providing enough length.
That's quite the opposite, usually.
It's like, all right, chill out.
Let's focus here.
Let's talk a little bit about quality, because you mentioned quality a couple of times.
What do you look for when you're doing a quality review of piece of content and then kind of articulating feedback?
What's your rubric there?
Yeah, so do you mean articulating feedback to another person or just quality review on my own work?
Let's talk about quality review of your own and then articulation to another person.
think those are two separate questions, but interrelated.
Yeah, so I think quality for me, the content needs to be.
I think the number one priority is that it needs to be helpful and valuable to a human reader.
Like I know right now, especially there's this like focus on how do we make content optimized for AI search.
And I think that's really important, of course, especially when it comes to visibility in these tools.
But ultimately, any content that I produce needs to offer something valuable to the reader that they maybe didn't think of before or couldn't find elsewhere.
So something that I like to include in my work are like real world examples or even just hypothetical scenarios of how to do something.
So rather than just saying, oh, you should do this, I would say, for example, if you want to find new users for your product, you can look at these online communities.
These are forums in your industry that might be helpful for you.
And that gives people like a jumping off point rather than like, oh, well, I know I can look online to look for new users.
But what would that look like?
So, yeah, number one, being like helpful and valuable to readers and kind of related to that is, again, being specific rather.
And just kind of like vague best practices or ideas, which people, if they think enough about something, could come up with on their own.
Giving people like a very clear next step of what they could do after reading this.
Like, oh, now that I have an understanding of, like one client we work with is an online marketplace builder.
So now I have an understanding of like how to build an MVP of my marketplace.
What are some of maybe the features this needs to include?
I can't, I don't need to include every single feature and be one of my product, but these are the ones that we need to include.
And why, especially if maybe there are some that are specific to certain types of marketplaces that they should consider that maybe other marketplaces might not.
So being specific in that sense.
And then, of course, in terms of like what's helpful for the client, like how is this content and the way that we write about it really supporting their goals or just their overall messaging?
So kind of like going back to like you.

Using AI to do SERP analysis or competitor research or things like that, like, of course, you can come up with tons of good ideas, things that maybe I used to do manually before, just clicking links and taking notes on my own.
So you have, like, this new wealth of information, but then the next step is how do you decide what you will use in your own ideas or your own content?

So, like, let's say it gives you, like, 10 suggestions of different sections to include in an article and why, like, what these sections should include, why you should include them, and then it's kind of like an editorial decision of, okay, I could, well, option one is include all these sections just to make sure everything is covered, but that can be, like, really scattered and all over the place.
And again, going back to human readers, I think people, first of all, are not going to read a super long article and it therefore won't be helpful to them.
And second of all, the other option is, okay, to make, like, more strategic editorial decisions of, okay, I know my target audience wants this, they're struggling.
These are the questions they tend to ask.

Now, how can I pick certain sections that my research has suggested to me, and then also add what I know about the client, what I know about the target audience, and then build something that takes into account what competitors do or what people are looking for when they're researching online, but also is very specific to the client's brand, their vision, their overall messaging, and what their product is able to do.

So I think it's a combination of serving the reader in, you know, very specific ways, rather than just, because again, I think, especially now, with all these AI search tools, you can look up any sort of top of the funnel question, and it's answered for you in like AI overviews, or chat GPT, or whatever, it needs to go, content needs to go like deeper than that.
And a lot of the time, that's not something that you can find just through online research, like ideally, you would have proprietary data, or interviews, or something from a client where you have
Have unique information that people genuinely can't find elsewhere and incorporate that into content.
And I think that just makes quality better.
It's more helpful to the reader.
And it is just better for the client because it really showcases that they have this authority that other brands don't, their competitors don't.
Yeah, that's great.
Yeah, I think that's all good.
I mean, obviously, there are many ways that you can answer that question that are right.
You know, there's no one right way.

But I also do think that this is, it's hewing towards similar conclusions that we have come to and that we come to because we're entering a weird era where measurement is about to change, you know, the measurement of goals.
How do you want to, like, scream about SEO over the past two decades?
Like, it has at least been some sort of benchmark, you know, that you can point to.
Winning or losing traffic here.
Where now, as you know, as well as I, there's been an enormous chunk of SEO content that's actually unhelpful to humans and unreadable.
Yeah.
That's like, we are at a weird inflection point here where measuring anything might become moot and is really kind of going back towards vibes and value, you know?
Yeah.

How do you write in a way where it's like, hey, we're kind of delivering on the promise, literally not clickbaiting people, we're delivering on the promise of, you know, the premise, aka headline or search results or whatever.

And offering it up in a way where it can benefit the client, brand, whatever, just as much as if it shows up in an answer as an authoritative source of information as it does if somebody goes, all right, you know what, I'm going to visit the website and get a little deeper on this.
Like those the two new venues rather than, oh, they land on the webpage and we need to hook them, you know, in a certain way with the introductory text.
So, what I'm hoping selfishly as the next...
Journalist and storyteller is that it really just comes back down to telling good stories again.
And all of the BS for the last 10 years where people were like, well, it's about good storytelling.
No, it's not.
It's about feeding Google exactly what it wants.
the algorithms.
Yeah, exactly.

So we've got a whole new set of algorithms to work with, but they are, thankfully, I think more, they're more likely to be friendly to human readable content, I believe, because they have been trained on human readable content, right?
Yeah, exactly.
to like Google, which is like, hey, we are going to determine what we actually care about.
And it may not be what, like, it may not have anything to do with this is actually a readable piece of content that is cool or good or fun or clever or whatever.
It may not have, it may have buckets to do with that.
And instead, it's driven by our needs to serve ads and to do everything else, And monetize, yeah.
Yeah.
So anyhow, it's an interesting era to enter into.
And I think...
Articulating that way is good.
I do have a personal bias towards human-readable content that actually feels like quality.
And that's part of my job here, is to make sure that whatever else our workflows are doing, that they are actually producing content that feels like quality just to our clients, right?
If we're using fabrics in our t-shirts and hoodies that feels cheap, they're going to be like, what is this?
What kind of company are we working with here?
And so it has to feel texturally sound and good stuff coming out the other end.
Yeah, it's a good way.
I like the t-shirt analogy or hoodie analogy.
Yeah, probably personal bias.
And then lastly, just kind of want to hear a little bit about what kind of work gets you energized and what do you really love?
What's the most favorite kind of work you do?
Because content work like this and workflow work does sometimes offer repetitive or technical tasks that you're doing consistently.
Mm So...
kind of curious how you stay engaged and what energizes you.
What do you find most interesting about that kind of work?
Yeah, I would say two main things.

The first being I really love actually getting to meet with clients and talk to them or interview subject matter experts and talk to real people about their industry or whatever topics they're interested in.
And I think part of that is like by nature of working in an industry, I don't have the opportunity to like work full time in some sort of industry or field of interest.
And so they are my best opportunity for learning more about what their world is like, especially I think working with clients or subject matter experts.
They're so they're like in their world all the time.
They maybe don't realize that there are some interesting questions or things that would be helpful to other people.
And so having the opportunity to talk to clients or interview subject matter experts, it really helps me in terms of like understanding what the client's goals are and coming up with.
Strategies and content that would be helpful for them rather than just only focusing on research or content production.

mean, of course that's useful too, but I think the thing that really helps me come up with new ideas or strategies that are helpful for clients is being able to talk with them and build that relationship where, you know, you're earning that trust and they understand that you're just trying to do everything to help them.

And, you know, prove to them that like the time that you spend meeting with me or that the money that you pay us without obviously saying it that way, it's like worth spending because the more I understand about you and the more that we do for you, we're able to, you know, get more context, build that relationship and that allows us to serve them better.
So I would say that's something that I get really excited about is just like meeting with people.
I'm also extroverted, so I think that definitely plays a big part of it.
And then the other thing is I really enjoy building processes that solve problems, whether it's for my team.
Okay.
A lot of the time it's for myself because it's easier to like experiment with one person.
But there have definitely been times where I've, you know, built workflows or processes for doing something.
then after figuring out, you know, getting through the speed bumps or figuring out what works and what doesn't, sharing that with my team to help them maybe in their lives.

And I think just having more standardized processes or workflows or whatever you want to call it just makes everyone's lives easier and also really helps with controlling the quality across the board.
So rather than just having everyone have their own process for doing something, being able to like improve a workflow or build a new process entirely, I think it just is really satisfying to me.
And it's, I enjoy, you know, like working with others and getting feedback in that sense.
Like, does this work or not?
Are you coming across, like, is this as high quality for you as I found it?
Maybe it doesn't work for every single use case or every single client or industry or something like

So that's something that I've done a lot in my role at Animals just kind of like out of my own personal interest honestly and because it makes my life easier and then I like sharing that with my team and so that's definitely something that I think really interests me about this opportunity is that is a huge part of this role is like actually getting to you know start like build workflows and have an impact on something that helps a bunch of other people not just like me coming up with processes to help myself.

Yeah that makes sense that's good I mean it's it's certainly a um it's a bonus when that uh that's like a pop sweetener for you because it's a lot of that you know the yeah my role is a lot of that but so are the Me's you know they need to like be able to articulate the process and improve it and then get that that spirit boost that juice from going like cool downstream from here I can see the immediate effects of that yeah lives are made easier they're delivering better faster all that

Yeah, and I think just by nature of like my current role, that's just not necessarily what I always had the time to do, but still being able to practice that on my own or over the past couple of years experimenting and just the opportunity to do that on a larger scale and being able to, like you said, like see those downstream effects more immediately.

I think even just myself being able to iterate and improve with my limitations, just like my time and my responsibilities, I think with the opportunity to do more, like I know you said, it's like a 60-40 split between workflow and content.
I think that would just allow me to do so much more and iterate more quickly and I think just improve.
And I feel like I've already, that's just something I was interested in already from like, I think about like two years ago when I started really experimenting with this.
So that's something I'm really excited about.
Great.
Yeah, sounds good.
Thank you very kindly for your time.
I appreciate it.
And I really hope reach back out with next steps.
All right.
Thank you so much, Matthew.
Thanks, Natalie.
Cool to meet you.
Ciao.
Nice to meet you as well.

## Meeting Purpose

[Hiring manager screen for Technical Managing Editor role at growthx.ai with candidate Nathalie Schrans.](https://fathom.video/share/54t1Wwq94VKryuVSQDyscpxWEfk6x-sB?tab=summary&timestamp=24.0)

## Key Takeaways

  - [Nathalie has 10 years of marketing experience, with 6+ years in content marketing, primarily B2B SaaS](https://fathom.video/share/54t1Wwq94VKryuVSQDyscpxWEfk6x-sB?tab=summary&timestamp=108.0)
  - [She has 2.5 years of experience with AI tools and workflows, including prompt engineering and quality control](https://fathom.video/share/54t1Wwq94VKryuVSQDyscpxWEfk6x-sB?tab=summary&timestamp=186.0)
  - [The role is \~60% workflow engineering, 40% content, requiring strong articulation of process improvements](https://fathom.video/share/54t1Wwq94VKryuVSQDyscpxWEfk6x-sB?tab=summary&timestamp=463.0)
  - [growthx.ai's approach involves treating workflows as "writers" with human oversight and continuous improvement](https://fathom.video/share/54t1Wwq94VKryuVSQDyscpxWEfk6x-sB?tab=summary&timestamp=419.0)

## Topics

### Candidate Background and Experience
